_full_text:
Summary of Gender-Based Analysis (GBA) Plus assessment
For project: Passport Program Facial Recognition System
Launch date: 2010
Project Overview
Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC) issues Canadian passports, refugee
travel documents, and certificates of identity documents. Facial recognition technology is a
tool that detects and analyzes the facial features of a person’s face to assist in the
identification and verification of an individual’s identity. A facial recognition system converts
facial images into facial biometric templates and stores them within the system for future
comparison. The system can compare a facial biometric against a collection of facial
biometrics resulting in a list of candidates ranked by a computer evaluated similarity score;
this is called a one-to-many identification. It can also conduct a verification by comparing two
facial biometrics in the same claimed identity against one another; this is called a one-to-one
verification. IRCC uses facial recognition technology to compare photos submitted with an
application for a Canadian passport or travel document against photos from past applications
held in our database. This helps IRCC quickly confirm that the applicant is the same person
who was issued a passport before. It also helps identify if someone has applied for multiple
passports, or has been flagged in our system as someone who should be denied a passport by
court order or another reason under the Canadian Passport Order. The tool supports identity
and passport entitlement assessments helping to streamline passport processing, detect
fraud, and keep Canadians safe. It does not make final decisions to approve or refuse a
passport application.
Objective and Scope of GBA Plus Analysis
In 2025, IRCC completed a gender-based analysis plus (GBA Plus) study. The study aimed to
assess how different populations may be impacted by the departments use of the facial
recognition system and determine whether any Canadian passport and travel document
applicant cohorts are disproportionately impacted by the system due to their gender or age.
We did this by testing how well the facial recognition system was able to identify paired
facial biometrics of male and females, aged sixteen years or older, who had applied for a
passport or travel document. Children under the age of sixteen were excluded because they
are not systematically compared against the facial recognition system when their application
is being processed. We also broadly examined human facial comparison and decision-making
processes that support the facial recognition system.
1
Methodology
Facial recognition systems use algorithms to detect faces, create facial biometric templates, and
compare and evaluate facial biometrics. The accuracy of an algorithm can be assessed by testing
to see how often it can correctly match two facial biometrics, that are known to be the same
person, when it is presented with many faces for comparison. Ideally the algorithm would find
the known matching face (Mated Pair) most similar to the facial biometric being tested (Probe)
and list the Mated Pair first in the candidate result list. A Rank 1 position means the algorithm
found that face to be the most similar to the Probe out of all of the other faces compared.
We used this method to test our algorithm’s performance using a data set of over two million
facial biometrics of adult applicants. These biometrics were accompanied by disaggregated data
to isolate populations for analysis (e.g. gender, age, date application received). Men and women
were equally represented in the data sample. We used 300 thousand facial biometrics as Probes
to compare against 300 thousand Mated Pairs and over 1.4 million singular (Non-Mated) facial
biometrics. Using known Mated Pairs and Non-Mated facial biometrics further challenged the
algorithm to successfully identify the right Mated Pair as most similar to the Probe rather than
incorrectly identifying another person’s face as more similar during a one-to-many comparison.
The whole data sample was used to test performance based on gender alone however, in order
to maintain gender equivalency of the sample, only a subset of data was used for tests restricted
by age group, elapsed time between image capture, and intersectional factors.
Limitations
The scope of this study was limited to assessing how the departments use of the facial
recognition system may impact different clients based on basic demographic attributes of
gender and age. Unfortunately, we were unable to assess a few other important factors due to
data constraints. As an example, passport clients who do not identify exclusively as female or
male may choose another gender identifier. However, this population was not included because
there wasn’t a large enough amount of data available for testing. Race or skin tone also wasn’t
addressed because this information isn’t collected from clients and no other data source was
available. Finally, while we explored issues that can impact human operators’ facial comparison
skills and decision-making, we did not test the accuracy of their decisions. IRCC is exploring ways
to address these limitations for future gender-based analysis plus activities.
2
Summary of Results
This assessment demonstrated that the facial recognition algorithm is highly accurate and that
both the system and the supporting processes do not pose significant inequity or have
negative impacts on clients based on gender or age differences. Results of performance
testing below reflect the proportion of Mated Pairs correctly matching at Rank 1, meaning the
algorithm identified the pair as most similar or the best match. Importantly, in passport’s
operational environment, not matching at rank 1 does not equate to not matching at all.
Tests showed, on average, the algorithm successfully matched the Probe to its known Mated
Pair more than 99.8% of the time at Rank 1. This means that, on average, the algorithm
incorrectly identified two different individuals as being more similar to one another than the
known Mated Pair at a rate of less than 0.2% or about 1 in 500 facial biometrics tested. For
gender, results showed the algorithm is slightly less accurate when comparing faces of women
to women rather than men to men. The difference of 0.05%, meant that for every 2000 facial
biometrics of women-women compared, we can expect there could be one more mismatch
than if performing the same test on men-men.
Test results also showed the algorithm is highly accurate, regardless of the adult’s age or the
time that has elapsed between when the biometrics were captured and differentials based on
these factors are minimal. The algorithm is slightly less accurate when comparing faces of
youth and young adults and match rates steadily increase as people age. Results also showed
matching accuracy was similar when comparing facial biometrics taken at five or eleven-year
intervals. Looking at performance when gender and age were combined, the algorithm was
least accurate when verifying facial biometrics of young women aged 26-35. This is not
surprising given females and younger clients had slightly poorer results when gender and age
were viewed on their own. This age group also showed the greatest difference in performance
based on gender.
Importantly, all applicant photos that fail the facial recognition verification are reviewed by
a human operator for decision and, both the system and processes, are designed to mitigate
risks of cognitive bias during human interventions. Decisions are never based solely on
facial recognition results and any decision to not issue a passport or travel document is
preceded by an entitlement review investigation and administrative decision-making
process.
3
Ongoing Monitoring & Next Steps
IRCC will continue to monitor the facial recognition system output as it relates to passport
applicant outcomes and use test results to form a performance baseline that considers
demographic differentials. IRCC is committed to updating this GBA Plus to address limitations
of this assessment once data and standards become available. For example, by compiling data
and assessing the facial recognition system’s impact on applicants who self-declare as another
gender and identifying the best methods to collect and analyze data to reflect an applicant’s
race or skin tone. In addition, IRCC will explore ways to further assess human intervention
processes and decisions that support the facial recognition system.
ring & Next Steps
